title: The Cost of Disorganization
intro: Your team needs to add a simple feature: showing the user's name in the navigation bar. It should take 30 minutes. Imagine needing your passport tomorrow but your room is a disaster -- a 10-second task becomes a 3-hour search. It's been three days and the feature still isn't shipped. Let's find out why disorganized code turns simple tasks into nightmares.

teach.title: Messy Code = Messy Room
teach.body: You need your passport for a trip tomorrow. In an organized room, you walk to the desk drawer and grab it in 10 seconds. In a messy room, you spend 3 hours digging through piles, checking pockets, and looking under furniture. **Disorganized code** is the messy room -- finding and changing anything takes 10 times longer than it should.

teach.title: Disorganized Code = Tangled Christmas Lights
teach.body: Every December you pull out the Christmas lights and find them in a tangled ball. You spend an hour untangling them before you can even start decorating. Now imagine if you'd stored them neatly on a reel last year -- you'd be decorating in minutes. **Disorganized code is the tangled ball of lights**: every time you need to make a change, you spend most of your time untangling before you can do any real work.

teach.title: The Real Costs
teach.body:
Disorganized code costs your team in four ways:

1. **Search time** -- 'Where does the user data come from?' becomes a 30-minute archeological dig
2. **Fear of change** -- 'If I change this, what else breaks?' leads to workarounds instead of fixes
3. **Onboarding time** -- New developers take weeks instead of days to contribute
4. **Duplicate bugs** -- The same logic in 5 places means fixing a bug in one place leaves 4 copies broken

teach.title: When Finding Code Takes Longer Than Writing It
teach.code:
src/
  app.js              (2,400 lines - everything)
  helpers.js          (800 lines - random utility functions)
  stuff.js            (350 lines - more random functions)
  components.js       (1,200 lines - all React components)
  api.js              (600 lines - all API calls mixed together)
teach.annotation: Where would you find the code that fetches the current user's name? You'd have to search all 5,350 lines across 5 vaguely-named files. In a well-organized project, you'd look in src/api/user.js or src/hooks/useCurrentUser.js.

teach.body: When you build apps with AI, disorganized code means every new feature request from a client takes 5x longer. Organized code lets you say 'yes' to more client work and ship faster -- that directly translates to more revenue per hour.

Disorganized code turns 30-minute tasks into 3-day ordeals. The real cost is search time, fear of changes, slow onboarding, and duplicate bugs. Good organization is an investment that pays back on every future change.

start.narrative:
Task: Display the logged-in user's name in the navigation bar. Expected time: 30 minutes.

You open the codebase and find a single file called app.js with 2,400 lines. There's also helpers.js (800 lines), stuff.js (350 lines), and components.js (1,200 lines). Where do you start looking for user data?

choices.text: Search for 'user' across all files to find where user data is handled
Start reading app.js from the top since it's the main file
Check helpers.js since getting user data sounds like a helper function

search-user.narrative: Searching for 'user' returns 147 results across all files. There's 'user', 'userData', 'currentUser', 'userInfo', 'usr', and 'loggedInUser' -- all meaning slightly different things in different places. Some are the same data fetched differently. Which do you trust?

choices.text: Find the login/auth code and trace forward to identify the source of truth
Pick the variable name that appears most often and assume it's canonical
Just grab the first 'currentUser' I see and use it

read-app.narrative: You read app.js line by line. 45 minutes later, you're at line 600 and have found database setup, routing, middleware, authentication, and socket handlers all jumbled together. The user logic is somewhere in these 2,400 lines but there are no sections or comments to guide you. You give up reading sequentially.

choices.text: Switch to searching the codebase for 'user' to jump to the right area

check-helpers.narrative: helpers.js has 47 functions with names like doThing(), process(), handle(), and format2(). There's a getCurrentUser() but it returns different data than getUserInfo() in stuff.js. Nobody documented which one is correct. You can't tell from this file alone.

choices.text: Search all files for 'user' to see the full picture

trace-truth.narrative: You find the source of truth: fetchUser() on line 1,847 of app.js calls the API on login and stores the result in a local variable 'usr' inside an IIFE. The nav component (line 342 of components.js) lives in a different module entirely and has no access to that local. How do you bridge them?

choices.text: Lift fetchUser into a UserContext provider and consume it from the nav with useUser()
Export the user variable from app.js and import it in the nav component
Attach the user object to window.usr so any file can read it anywhere

scope-control.narrative: Now think about staleness. After login, fetchUser runs once and caches the result. If the user updates their name later in /settings, the nav still shows the old name until refresh. How do you handle updates?

choices.text: Make UserContext re-fetch on profile-save events and broadcast the new value to all consumers
Force a full page reload after profile-save -- simple and guaranteed fresh
Tell users to refresh manually if their name looks wrong

prevent-future.narrative: The feature ships. While you have the codebase loaded, what (if anything) do you do to prevent the next 30-minute task from also taking 3 days?

choices.text: Move auth/user code into src/auth/ and src/hooks/useUser.ts so the next person finds it immediately, and add a 5-line README of the data flow
Add a one-line comment near fetchUser pointing to UserContext and move on
Nothing -- the ticket is closed, refactoring is somebody else's problem

end-good.narrative: The feature works AND the codebase is incrementally cleaner. The next teammate looking for user data finds src/auth/useUser.ts in 10 seconds. A 30-minute task next time will actually take 30 minutes. You paid back a small chunk of the technical debt that delayed you.
end-good.feedback: This is the boy-scout rule in action: leave the campsite a little cleaner than you found it. The big-bang refactor that 'fixes everything' never ships, but a small, scoped cleanup tied to a real ticket always does. Disorganization compounds; so do small cleanups.

end-mediocre.narrative: The feature works. The next person looking for user data still has to grep across 5,350 lines, but at least when they find fetchUser they'll see your comment pointing to UserContext. You saved 5 minutes of someone's future search time and not much more.
end-mediocre.feedback: A breadcrumb is better than nothing, but it doesn't change the underlying structure -- the codebase is still a maze. Comments rot; structure scales. The next time you touch this area, prefer moving code into a clearly-named file over leaving a note about where it lives.

end-weak.narrative: Ticket closed in 4 hours. Two weeks later, a new hire is asked to add the user's avatar next to their name. They spend a day repeating the exact same archaeological dig you just did, and probably introduce yet another 'currentUser' variable in the process.
end-weak.feedback: Doing nothing is the choice that keeps the cycle going. You had the context loaded and the proof of cost (3 days for a 30-minute task) right in front of you -- that was the cheapest moment to spend 20 minutes on cleanup. Skipping it ensures the next feature pays the full price again.
